ai_review_item.review_item:
The manuscript’s analysis choices (simulation-fixed fit shapes, external calibration inputs, and pseudoexperiment-based uncertainties) are not documented in enough detail, and are not accompanied by reusable released artifacts, to enable independent reproduction or reanalysis of the key result.

"The signal shape is modelled as a combination of a Gaussian and two Crystal Ball functions [49], all sharing the same peak position. The parameters of the signal function are determined from simulated events and are fixed in the fit to data, except for the Gaussian width, the average width of the Crystal Ball functions, and the peak position, which accounts for imperfections in the simulation." 
This shows that the extracted yields (and thus the asymmetry) depend on simulation-derived shape parameters that are not fully floated in the data fit. For an external reader, reproducing the measurement requires access not only to the data but also to the exact simulation configuration, the derived fixed parameter values, and the fitting implementation. The manuscript does not tabulate these fixed parameters or provide a machine-readable specification of the full likelihood model, making an independent reproduction infeasible even in principle. This is not merely a documentation issue: if fixed parameters shift yields differently for baryon and antibaryon due to subtle reconstruction differences, the asymmetry can be biased. Providing the parameter values and a compact statistical model description (or a public likelihood artifact) would significantly strengthen reproducibility and facilitate scrutiny.
"The Durham High Energy Physics Database (HEPData) has been built up over the past four decades as a unique open-access repository for scattering data from experimental particle physics papers. It comprises data points underlying several thousand publications." ([[3]](#ref3))
This reference illustrates an established community mechanism for releasing the numerical content underlying published results in particle physics. For a paper making a historically significant first observation claim, depositing the key measurement tables (e.g., asymmetries, uncertainties, and possibly binned distributions needed for reinterpretation) in such repositories is a common and impactful practice. The manuscript does not indicate any such deposit or provide equivalent machine-readable artifacts for the measurement, which limits downstream re-use and independent cross-checking. While raw LHCb collision data may not be trivially shareable, the derived high-level results and auxiliary tables often are. The absence of an explicit artifact release therefore materially harms reproducibility under common open-science norms in HEP.
"CERN today launched its Open Data Portal where data from real collision events, produced by experiments at the Large Hadron Collider (LHC) will for the first time be made openly available to all." ([[4]](#ref4))
This CERN policy statement demonstrates that LHC data and accompanying tools can, in some circumstances, be released openly and in a reusable form. While not all analyses can be reproduced from open data today, the existence of such infrastructure raises the bar for transparency around what is (or is not) being shared for a particular result. The manuscript provides no data/code availability statement and no pointers to reusable analysis materials, leaving readers unable to assess what is needed to reproduce the published asymmetry. Even if full reproduction is restricted to the collaboration, providing analysis-level outputs (tables, simplified likelihoods, or open code for the statistical treatment) would enable partial independent validation. The external quote supports the expectation that major LHC results should, where feasible, provide clearer pathways for data and tool sharing.

References:
[3] [E. Maguire, L. Heinrich, G. Watt, HEPData: a repository for high energy physics data (arXiv:1704.05473)](https://arxiv.org/pdf/1704.05473)
[4] [CERN, CERN makes public first data of LHC experiments (Open Data Portal announcement)](https://home.cern/news/news/accelerators/cern-makes-public-first-data-lhc-experiments)

expert_comment.text: The main point of the critique is correct. But to the bacterial modeling community this paper does not read that it claims a universal law. However, the AI reviewer is correct with the statement "The paper would need clearer limits on scope (e.g., "within these assembled microcosms and this dispersal/invasion protocol") or additionaltests demonstrating the relationship persists under qualitatively different invasion settings." It is harsh, but correct request.

ai_review_item.review_item:
This paper makes an overgeneralized claim (Validity; Conclusions) that invasion probability universally collapses onto survival fraction across conditions, but the main theoretical support relies on an exchangeable generalized Lotka–Volterra setup (identical intrinsic parameters and randomly drawn interactions) that can mechanically enforce such a collapse, and the experiments do not establish universality beyond the studied library and protocol.

“Despite these conflicting diversity-invasibility relationships, after scaling richness with species pool size to get the survival fraction, we found that all communities collapsed to a universal line in which the invasion probability is approximately equal to the survival fraction (Fig. 4b).”
This sentence asserts a universal relationship, but the paper’s own phrasing indicates the collapse is obtained after a specific rescaling (richness divided by pool size), not from an independently validated mechanism. A collapse can occur for multiple reasons, including shared definitions and exchangeability assumptions, without implying universality across ecological contexts. The manuscript does not provide out-of-distribution validation (e.g., different taxa, interaction types, or invasion protocols) to justify the “universal” framing. Because later conclusions extrapolate this relationship to natural communities, the evidentiary standard should be higher than a within-system collapse. The paper would need clearer limits on scope (e.g., “within these assembled microcosms and this dispersal/invasion protocol”) or additional tests demonstrating the relationship persists under qualitatively different invasion settings.
“For simplicity and without qualitatively changing our results, we considered the same growth rate \( r_i = 1 \) and the same carrying capacity \( K_i = 1 \) for all species in the main text.”
Setting all intrinsic growth rates and carrying capacities equal makes species effectively exchangeable except through the random interaction matrix, which strengthens symmetry-based relationships between resident survival and a “new” species’ invasion probability. Under such exchangeability, the probability that an added species persists can become tightly linked to the fraction of species that persist from the same parameter distribution. This means the reported collapse may partially reflect model construction rather than an empirically grounded ecological law. The manuscript does not quantify how sensitive the claimed universality is to realistic heterogeneity in \(r_i\), \(K_i\), or interaction structure beyond citing prior work. Given that microbial interactions are often mediated by environmental modification and can be strongly context-dependent, these simplifications matter for the validity of universal claims. At minimum, the paper should show the collapse persists under broader, empirically motivated parameter heterogeneity and/or mechanistic mediation.
```matlab
r = ones(S, 1); % Growth rates (set to 1 for simplicity)
AA = rand(S, S)*A*2;
AA = AA - diag(diag(AA)) + eye(S); % Ensure diagonal elements are 1
...
AA(S, :) = (rand(1, S) + 0.5) * A;
AA(:, S) = (rand(S, 1) + 0.5) * A;
```
This code implements the exchangeability noted in the text: identical intrinsic growth rates and a randomly sampled interaction matrix for all species, including the invader. The invader’s interaction coefficients are drawn from the same strength scale \(A\) as the residents, which makes “invader vs resident” a label rather than a qualitatively different ecological role. In such a setup, it is unsurprising that invasion success statistics can align with resident survival statistics, because both are governed by the same parameter-generating process. This weakens the interpretation that a collapse reveals a universal, community-level predictor rather than a consequence of how the model was parameterized. If the paper’s universality claim is meant to apply to real invasions (often involving species with systematically different traits or interaction mechanisms), the model should test non-exchangeable invaders (e.g., shifted \(r\), \(K\), or interaction distributions). Without that, the link between the modeled collapse and the empirical “universal” claim remains under-justified.
“Pairwise models are commonly used to describe many-species communities. In these models, an individual receives additive fitness effects from pairwise interactions with each species in the community ('additivity assumption'). All pairwise interactions are typically represented by a single equation where parameters reflect signs and strengths of fitness effects ('universality assumption'). Here, we show that a single equation fails to qualitatively capture diverse pairwise microbial interactions.” ([1](#ref1))
This external evidence highlights a known limitation of relying on a single Lotka–Volterra-type pairwise equation form as a universal description for microbial interactions. The reviewed paper’s “universal correspondence” is derived in large part from such a gLV framework, while the experiments themselves involve media modification (e.g., pH) and dispersal that can create higher-order or mediated effects. If gLV’s universality/additivity assumptions fail in many microbial settings, then a “universal” law inferred from gLV requires extra empirical validation rather than being taken as broadly applicable. The manuscript does not directly address whether the mechanisms generating its experimental priority effects and fluctuations are compatible with pairwise-additive gLV dynamics in the relevant regimes. This undermines the strength of conclusions that elevate a model-derived collapse to a general predictive principle. A stronger approach would triangulate with mechanistic mediation models or demonstrate that inferred pairwise dynamics accurately predict unseen invasion scenarios.

expert_comment.text: I think the AI does understand how hard ecology is! R2 of 0.36 is impressive!

ai_review_item.review_item:
The models explaining biodiversity responses to yield have marginal pseudo-R² values ranging from 0.05 to 0.36 (with fixed effects explaining only 5% to 36% of variance), and the authors acknowledge that their "models do not fully fit model assumptions," yet these weakly predictive models are used to project biodiversity changes globally, generating estimates of species losses up to 89% that should be interpreted with substantial uncertainty.

"The variance explained by fixed effects (marginal pseudo-R²) for all models varied between 0.05 and 0.36, whereas the variance explained by fixed and random effects together (conditional pseudo-R²) varied between 0.52 and 0.98 (Nakagawa & Schielzeth 2013)."
A marginal R² of 0.05 means that the fixed effects (including yield and its interactions) explain only 5% of the variance in biodiversity outcomes, with the remaining 47-95% explained by random effects (study-level variation, spatial blocks). While low marginal R² is common in PREDICTS-based analyses, using such models to project global biodiversity changes generates predictions where most of the variance is driven by factors not captured in the projections (since random effects cannot be extrapolated beyond the sampled studies). The projection of 89.1% maximum species loss relies on these weakly predictive relationships.
"The model diagnostic plots suggest that our models do not fully fit model assumptions (Figure S5 – S21)."
The authors acknowledge that model assumptions are violated but do not discuss the implications for their projections. Violation of model assumptions can lead to biased parameter estimates and unreliable prediction intervals. When such models are then used for global extrapolation (projecting yield gap closure effects), the compounded uncertainty from poor model fit and low explained variance may render the quantitative estimates unreliable.
"These levels of pseudo-R² are typical for models based on datasets collected from multiple source, in which most of the variation is explained by the random effects (Williams et al. 2022)."
While low marginal R² may be typical for PREDICTS analyses, this does not resolve the problem for making projections. The high conditional R² (0.52-0.98) comes from random effects that capture study-level differences in sampling methods, taxonomic focus, and geographic region—none of which can be projected onto new locations. The projections essentially apply the weak fixed-effect relationships (5-36% explained variance) to global agricultural landscapes, generating quantitative estimates that appear precise but have substantial unquantified uncertainty.

ai_review_item.review_item:
This paper’s core experimental outcome measures (Validity; Conclusions) treat invasion success and priority effects as emergent community properties, but the protocol includes continued dispersal/immigration of the invader and residents after invasion, so “invasion success” is not cleanly separated from sustained propagule pressure and may not reflect intrinsic community resistance or true priority effects.

“After six days of culturing, each community was exposed to an invader species (Fig. 1a) and we continued to culture the communities for another 6 days with dispersal of all species on each dilution cycle (Fig. 1a, b).”
This explicitly states that after the invader is introduced, dispersal of “all species” continues throughout the invasion assay. That design means the invader is not a one-time introduction; instead, it experiences ongoing immigration, which is a central driver of establishment in invasion ecology. Under such conditions, the measured “invasion probability” can reflect the imposed propagule pressure regime as much as community resistance mechanisms. This matters because the paper interprets differences between regimes (e.g., “priority effects” under strong interactions) as emergent outcomes of resident interactions, not as outcomes shaped by continued introductions. The manuscript does not present a parallel experiment with a single invader pulse (no subsequent invader dispersal) to show the main conclusions are robust to this distinction. As a result, the external validity of the conclusions for invasions without continuous immigration is unclear.
“After introducing the invaders on day 6, we performed another 6 daily dilution cycles with a \(10^{-5}\) dispersal rate for all species including the invader species until the end of the experiment.”
This sentence confirms that the invader is repeatedly reintroduced after day 6 through the same dispersal mechanism applied to residents. Such continuous input can maintain a population above a detection threshold even if it would otherwise go extinct, blurring the line between “establishment” and “presence under immigration.” Because the paper defines invasion success via a relative-abundance threshold at the end of the experiment, repeated reintroduction creates a direct pathway for classification outcomes to depend on the dispersal regime. The authors do not quantify how much of the observed invader signal could be explained by the dispersal rate relative to growth, especially for borderline successes. This is a major interpretability issue because the central claims compare invasion probabilities across regimes and attribute them to community dynamical state and interaction strength. A robustness check varying invader dispersal (or removing it after the initial pulse) would be needed to support the intended “invasibility” interpretation.
“We interpret the mismatch between invasion probability and survival fraction under high nutrient as evidence of priority effects in the community assembly under strong interactions in our experiment.”
The inference of priority effects is built on a deviation between two quantities, one of which (invasion probability) is measured under continued immigration and a specific invasion timing (day 6). Priority effects in the broader literature refer to order/timing dependence of assembly outcomes, which is typically demonstrated by manipulating arrival sequences and showing alternative endpoints. Here, the mismatch is used as indirect evidence, but the protocol’s ongoing dispersal and the lack of explicit sequence manipulations make it difficult to isolate “priority” from other causes (e.g., different effective propagule pressures, transient dynamics, or immigration–extinction balance). The paper does not show that introducing the same invader earlier vs later (or changing invader arrival order) changes outcomes in a way consistent with priority effects under the same dispersal regime. Without such direct tests, the conclusion that the mismatch constitutes priority effects is not robust. At minimum, the manuscript should explicitly label this as suggestive rather than evidentiary and provide targeted arrival-order experiments.
“The relationship between founding population size (often termed propagule pressure; …) and establishment success is sufficiently general that it is considered a “null model for biological invasions” …” ([2](#ref2))
This external statement summarizes a widely recognized principle: establishment success is strongly shaped by the number of introduced individuals and/or introduction frequency. The reviewed paper’s invasion assay includes both a sizable initial inoculation (“\(10^{-3}\)” volume ratio) and continued daily dispersal of the invader, which directly modulates propagule pressure. Therefore, interpreting invasion outcomes as emergent properties of resident interactions without explicitly modeling or varying propagule pressure risks conflating mechanisms. In particular, differences across nutrient and interaction regimes could change how immigration translates into detectable relative abundance, even if intrinsic resistance were unchanged. The manuscript would be strengthened by demonstrating that its key relationships (e.g., survival fraction predicting invasibility, priority-effect signatures) persist when propagule pressure is experimentally manipulated or controlled. Absent that, the conclusions about “invasibility” as a community property are less secure.

ai_review_item.review_item:
All molecular predictors (gene expression, receptor densities, metabolism, synapse density, myelination) and connectivity predictors are derived exclusively from healthy adult participants, yet these are used to predict and explain cortical abnormalities in patient populations where these features may be substantially altered by disease processes.

"Gene expression data was collected by the Allen Human Brain Atlas as described in Hawrylycz et al."
The Allen Human Brain Atlas is derived from postmortem brain tissue of donors without known neurological or psychiatric disorders. However, the disorders studied in this paper (schizophrenia, depression, epilepsy, Parkinson's disease, etc.) are known to involve altered gene expression patterns in the brain. Using healthy-derived gene expression to predict patient abnormalities assumes that the spatial pattern of gene expression is preserved in disease, which may not be valid for many of the studied conditions.
"Receptor densities were estimated using PET tracer studies for a total of 18 receptors and transporters, across 9 neurotransmitter systems."
The PET receptor density maps are from healthy volunteers, but many disorders studied involve receptor alterations. For example, dopamine receptors are altered in Parkinson's disease, schizophrenia, and ADHD; serotonin receptors are altered in depression and OCD. Using healthy receptor profiles to predict abnormalities in these disorders is circular if the diseases involve changes to the very receptor systems being used as predictors.
"The data used was structural and functional connectivity data for 69 healthy adults (25 males, 28.8 ± 9.1 years of age) from the Lausanne University Hospital"
The structural and functional connectomes used to assess "connectomic vulnerability" are from healthy young adults, yet diseases like schizophrenia, epilepsy, and Parkinson's are associated with altered structural and functional connectivity. Hansen et al. (2022) [[4]](#citation-list) themselves noted that "only seven proteins... show significant and large relationships with the expression of their corresponding genes," highlighting that even in healthy brains, the relationship between molecular measures can be complex. In disease states, these relationships may be further disrupted.

* Quote from the paper's limitations: "both local biological and global connectivity predictors are derived from state-of-the-art open-access datasets in healthy participants, but they do not capture individual variability or changes across the lifespan"
While the authors acknowledge this as a limitation, they do not discuss how disease-related changes to molecular and connectivity profiles might specifically bias their results. For example, if receptor densities are systematically altered in patient populations, the correlations observed between healthy receptor profiles and patient abnormalities may reflect confounding by regional vulnerability rather than causal mechanisms.

This paper does not provide sufficient information and runnable artifacts to reproduce its central quantitative outputs (complex dielectric function, radiative rate/lifetime, damping rate) derived from fitting s-SNOM spectra with the point dipole model.

"As shown in Figs. 2c,d, the point dipole model with Eq.1 as the input dielectric function provides an adequate fit to the data (see SOM for details on fitting)."
The paper’s core quantitative claims rely on a fitting/inversion procedure whose details are explicitly deferred to “SOM,” but the fitting workflow (objective function, constraints, priors, initialization, treatment of background, and handling of correlations among parameters) is not described in the main text. Without those details, an independent reader cannot reproduce Table 1 values or assess whether the fits are unique or robust to alternative model choices. This is especially problematic because the extracted parameters (e.g., oscillator strength via \(\gamma_{r,0}\), and damping via \(\gamma_{nr},\gamma_d\)) are sensitive to normalization, tip–sample coupling, and baseline assumptions in s-SNOM analyses. In common practice for quantitative near-field spectroscopy, authors provide either a full fitting recipe and sensitivity analysis in the main text/SI or share scripts and data that deterministically regenerate fit results. Here, the key fitting and uncertainty methodology is not recoverable from the manuscript alone. This directly undermines the validity of the extracted dielectric function and rates as reported.
"Our layer thickness assignment is further confirmed by the second harmonic generation (SHG) data (see supplementary Figs. S2 and S3)."
Several experimental validations that underpin the interpretation (e.g., layer-number assignment and twist-angle characterization) are referenced only via supplementary figures, but those materials are not available in the provided preprint package. For quantitative conclusions about heterobilayer screening and hybridization, correct layer identification and twist angle are not minor details; they are central to causal attribution. In established practice, such characterization is either included in the main text or provided in accessible supplementary materials so readers can verify the sample state used for each spectrum. Without access to those figures/data, readers cannot independently check whether the analyzed regions correspond to the stated monolayer/heterobilayer/trilayer configurations. This limits confidence in the downstream parameter extraction and comparisons across regions. The absence of the referenced supporting material materially impacts reproducibility and interpretability.
"By scanning the tip on the sample, complex near-field signal is recorded with a resolution of ~20 nm (Fig. 1b and supplementary Fig. S1)"
The asserted spatial resolution is used as a key bound in later inferences (e.g., screening-length upper bound), but the supporting evidence is again relegated to an unavailable supplementary figure. In near-field microscopy, “resolution” depends on tip radius, demodulation order, and analysis method, and it is typically demonstrated using a suitable edge response or calibration standard. Without the referenced evidence, the reader cannot verify the effective resolution achieved under the specific near-IR excitation and 5th-harmonic demodulation conditions used here. Because the paper later interprets spatial variations on the order of 20 nm, the absence of this validation is not cosmetic: it affects the soundness of spatially resolved claims. A reproducible study would provide the resolution demonstration and the calibration method used to define it. The current materials do not allow such verification.
"• TMDexcitonszSI.docx"
The manuscript indicates that the supplementary information exists as an external file, yet it is not included in the review package, leaving critical methodological and validation content inaccessible. When a paper’s main quantitative conclusions explicitly depend on supplementary methods/figures, omission of that file makes the results non-reproducible in practice for reviewers and readers. This is a significant barrier because the SI typically contains details such as fit residuals, parameter correlations, calibration steps, and additional datasets (e.g., full hyperspectral cubes) needed to validate model assumptions. Common publication standards in quantitative spectroscopy require that such supporting documents be available alongside the manuscript. Without the SI, readers cannot follow or audit the computational steps from measured near-field signals to dielectric function. This deficiency directly affects the reliability of the paper’s key reported numbers.
```python
import cPickle

from NearFieldOptics import Materials as mat
from common.misc import extract_array
from common.numerics import Spectrum
from NearFieldOptics import Inversion as inv
```
The provided code is not self-contained as written, because it imports packages/modules (e.g., `NearFieldOptics`, `common`) that are not present in the supplied code directory structure. As a result, a reader cannot run the inversion/fitting tooling “out of the box” to reproduce any of the manuscript’s fits without reconstructing an external, undocumented software environment. This is not a minor inconvenience: reproducibility of quantitative parameter extraction depends on executing the exact modeling and inversion steps. While it is acceptable to rely on external dependencies, common practice is to provide explicit installation instructions (e.g., `setup.py`/`pyproject.toml`, requirements, or environment files) and a clear entry point that reproduces figures and tables. The repository here lacks that packaging and dependency specification, making the computational pathway opaque. Consequently, the released code does not substantiate the reproducibility of the reported dielectric functions and rates.

ai_review_item.review_item:
The paper does not adequately discuss or correct for the known systematic underestimation of PM2.5 concentrations by CMIP6 models, particularly in highly polluted regions like China, which could bias DAPP projections.

"We calculated future the PM₂.₅ concentration in China based on empirical formulas and future pollutant concentration data"
The paper mentions using CMIP6 pollutant concentration data but does not discuss bias correction. Multiple studies have documented that CMIP6 models systematically underestimate surface PM2.5 concentrations, particularly in regions with high pollution levels like East Asia. This could lead to underestimation of future DAPP if not addressed.
"Turnock et al. reported that CMIP6 models underestimate the historical surface PM2.5 worldwide and projected the decrease of PM2.5 in future scenarios containing strong air quality and climate mitigation measures" [[5]](#citation-5)
The established literature, including Turnock et al. (2020), demonstrates that CMIP6 models underestimate historical PM2.5 concentrations globally. For China specifically, where PM2.5 levels are among the highest in the world, this underestimation bias could be substantial. The reviewed paper does not discuss how this known bias might affect its projections or apply bias correction methods commonly used in such studies.
"Disagreements in the projection of future changes to regional surface PM2.5 concentrations between individual CMIP6 models could be attributed to differences in the complexity of the aerosol schemes implemented within models" [[5]](#citation-5)
The intermodel variability in CMIP6 PM2.5 projections, as noted by Turnock et al., is driven by differences in aerosol representations across models. The reviewed paper uses 11 CMIP6 models but does not discuss which specific models were selected or whether the selection considered model performance in reproducing historical PM2.5 over China. This lack of discussion about model selection and bias correction is a methodological limitation.

References:
[5] [Turnock et al. (2020). Historical and future changes in air pollutants from CMIP6 models. Atmospheric Chemistry and Physics, 20, 14547-14579](https://acp.copernicus.org/articles/20/14547/2020/)

ai_review_item.review_item:
This paper’s conclusion that SurVIndel2 yields a substantially more complete 1000 Genomes CNV catalogue with “high validation rate” is insufficiently supported because the validation is reported only “in samples with long reads” that overlap the HGSVC2 cohort used to train and benchmark SurVIndel2’s filtering model, making the validation non-independent and potentially optimistic.

“The machine learning model of SurVIndel2 was trained using the 34 samples in HGSVC2 sampled by the NYGC. For this reason, when testing them, we use a leave-one-out strategy.”
Leave-one-out within the same 34-sample cohort reduces direct per-sample label leakage, but it does not provide an independent external validation of the learned filtering behavior. The training and evaluation data share the same sequencing center, platform, library preparation, and mapping pipeline characteristics, which the paper itself notes can create homogeneity and risk overfitting. When the same cohort is then used to compute “validation rates” for catalogue-level claims, the evaluation can become circular: the model is tuned to maximize agreement with long-read-derived calls in this cohort, and the reported validation is measured against those same long-read resources. This can inflate validation metrics relative to genuinely independent samples or platforms and makes it hard to extrapolate to the full 3,202-sample catalogue. A stronger design would validate the 1000 Genomes discoveries on long-read samples not used in model training/tuning, ideally sequenced and processed independently. Without that, “high validation rate” is not necessarily evidence of generalizable correctness.
“Between parentheses, the validation rates of calls in samples with long reads.”
This captioned evaluation explicitly restricts validation-rate reporting to a subset of samples with long-read data, rather than validating across the full cohort. If the long-read subset is largely the HGSVC2 set (as implied by the repeated use of HGSVC2 for benchmarking and validation throughout), then the validation statistic is computed on the same distribution used to build and evaluate the classifier. Even if many calls are rare, the selection of validated samples and genomic contexts can materially bias estimated validation upward or downward. For a population-scale callset claim, readers need to know how validation varies across populations, sequencing batches, and mapping contexts, not only within a curated long-read subset. The paper does not provide uncertainty estimates or stratified validation for the novel-event component (e.g., by repeat context, size, or allele frequency) in an independent validation cohort. As a result, the catalogue-level completeness claims are less robust than they appear.
“Furthermore, SurVIndel2 predicts 130,657 deletions not present in 1000g-SV, with high validation rate (0.87). … SurVIndel2 predicts an additional 197,008 duplications, with high validation rate (0.85).”
These large absolute numbers are a central conclusion of the paper, so the evidentiary standard should be particularly high. The validation rates are presented as though they generalize to the catalogue’s novel events, but the text ties validation to “samples with long reads,” which are a small fraction of the 3,202 genomes. If validation is computed only where long reads exist, it may systematically miss cohort-specific artifacts (e.g., platform- or mapping-related false positives) present elsewhere in the 3,202 sample set. Additionally, because the classifier was trained on the HGSVC2 cohort, agreement with long-read truth in those individuals is partly an expected consequence of the training objective, not an external check. Common practice in benchmarking emphasizes using test sets that were not used to train or optimize pipelines to avoid optimistic estimates of precision/recall. Without an external validation cohort, the conclusion that these hundreds of thousands of additional events are genuinely high-confidence remains insufficiently substantiated.
“Additionally, the pipelines that generated the variant calls could be tuned to HG001, which, in many situations, was used to train or optimize pipelines.” ([[1]](#ref1))
This observation from benchmarking best practices highlights a well-known pitfall: performance estimates can be inflated when a benchmark sample (or closely related sample cohort) is used to tune or train methods. The same risk applies here at the cohort level: SurVIndel2’s filtering model is trained on HGSVC2 samples, and catalogue validation appears to be judged using long-read data from the same or closely related HGSVC2 cohort. Even with leave-one-out, repeated model development decisions (feature design, filter thresholds, and model structure) can be influenced by aggregate performance on the benchmark cohort. This makes catalogue-level validation less independent than readers might assume. Independent validation (different sequencing centers/platforms, different individuals with orthogonal long-read truth) is typically needed to establish that high validation rates are not a byproduct of benchmark-specific tuning. The paper’s current evidence does not clearly meet that standard for the “hundreds of thousands” novel-event claim.

References:
[1] [Krusche et al., “Best Practices for Benchmarking Germline Small Variant Calls in Human Genomes,” *Nature Biotechnology* (2019)](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6699627/)

ai_review_item.review_item:
The comparison between NequIP (133 training structures) and DeepMD (133,500 training structures) in Table III does not constitute a valid data efficiency comparison because it compares different architectures at vastly different operating points rather than showing learning curves for both methods.

"A DeepMD NN-IP model was previously trained [15] for water and ice using a joint training set containing 133,500 reference calculations of these four systems. To assess data efficiency of the NequIP architecture, we similarly train a model jointly on all four parts of the data set, but using only 133 structures for training, i.e. 1000x fewer data."
While this comparison shows that NequIP achieves better force RMSE with 133 structures than DeepMD does with 133,500 structures, it does not actually demonstrate "data efficiency" in the proper sense. Data efficiency would require showing that NequIP consistently achieves lower errors than DeepMD at the same training set size, or equivalently, that NequIP reaches a target accuracy with fewer data points. The comparison as presented could simply mean that NequIP is a fundamentally better architecture regardless of data size, rather than being more "data-efficient."
"TABLE III: Root mean square error (RMSE) of force components on liquid water and the three ices in units of [meV/Å]. Note that the NequIP model was trained on < 0.1% of the training data of DeepMD."
A proper data efficiency study would train both NequIP and DeepMD on identical training set sizes (e.g., 10, 50, 100, 500, 1000, 10000 structures) and plot learning curves showing error as a function of training data size. This is precisely what the paper does for the l=0 vs l=0/l=1 comparison in Figure 7, but fails to do for the DeepMD comparison. Without this, the 1000x claim is scientifically imprecise because we cannot determine where the crossover point is or whether DeepMD might actually be more data-efficient at certain data regimes.
"The algorithm learns as new data become available. Empirical results have shown that this exponent typically remains fixed across different learning algorithms for a given data set, and different methods only shift the learning curve, leaving the log-log slope unaffected" [[1]](#citation-list)
The published Nature Communications version acknowledges that different methods often have similar scaling exponents, meaning they learn at similar rates as data increases. The preprint's comparison at a single point (133 vs 133,500) cannot capture this scaling behavior, which is essential for characterizing data efficiency.

ai_review_item.review_item:
This paper’s central “compound mortality impact” calculation is not methodologically valid because it adds temperature-attributable deaths and COVID-19 deaths under an explicit independence/additivity assumption, even though the manuscript itself presents evidence and prior literature that the two hazards can interact and share causal pathways; this affects the paper’s core results on heatwaves/cold snaps and the interpretation of “compound” burdens.

These temperature-related deaths are theoretically independent of COVID-19 deaths because they are calculated from DLNMs that describe the relationships between daily mean temperature and daily all-cause mortality, after COVID-19 mortality has been removed (see Methods).
Removing deaths with COVID-19 on the death certificate changes the outcome series, but it does not establish causal independence between temperature and pandemic conditions. COVID-19 can change population vulnerability, healthcare access, and reporting/diagnostic patterns, all of which can modify the temperature–mortality relationship even if COVID-coded deaths are excluded. In addition, some deaths “with COVID-19” could still be partly temperature-triggered, and excluding them can shift attributable fractions rather than eliminate overlap. The claim of theoretical independence is therefore stronger than what the described preprocessing supports. Because the paper’s main “compound” totals depend on this premise, any violation can materially bias compound magnitudes upward or downward. This is a validity issue because it affects the correctness of the main quantitative estimates.
By adding temperature-related deaths (with COVID-19 deaths removed prior to calculation) and COVID-19 deaths together to estimate the compound mortality impact, we have assumed that they are independent of each other.
This sentence confirms the compound metric is constructed by direct addition, which is only appropriate if the two components are non-overlapping and not interacting in ways that change attribution. The paper does not provide a joint model or an attribution framework that partitions deaths into mutually exclusive components (e.g., main effects plus interaction). Without such a framework, “compound” totals can double count overlapping contributions or miss interaction-driven excess risk. The manuscript later acknowledges potential confounding/effect modification (see next quotes), which directly contradicts treating the components as independent for estimation. In environmental epidemiology, compound-risk questions are typically addressed with interaction terms, stratified associations, or counterfactual decomposition rather than unconditional addition. As written, the compound totals are not a robust estimate of combined burden.
These results suggest that COVID-19 may have impacted temperature-related mortality during extreme weather events.
This admission implies effect modification or confounding: the temperature–mortality relationship (or baseline risk) differs between COVID and non-COVID periods. If COVID-19 alters temperature-related mortality, then the paper’s additive decomposition into “temperature deaths” plus “COVID deaths” is not stable across periods and cannot be interpreted as two separable components. In that case, a valid compound analysis needs to explicitly model pandemic-related modifiers (e.g., time-varying indicators, healthcare pressure proxies) and quantify uncertainty in the decomposition. Otherwise, the reported compound burdens during heatwaves/cold snaps conflate direct temperature effects, indirect pandemic effects, and their interaction. The figure-based KS test evidence the authors cite supports “difference,” but difference alone does not validate an additive partition. This undermines the conclusions that compound impacts are “clear” and “additive to the first order.”
Because of the common risk factors for temperature-related and COVID-19 mortality, disentangling their effects on health and attributing mortality to a single cause is challenging.
Shared risk factors imply that simple cause-wise partitioning is difficult and that overlap is expected, especially in older and comorbid populations. If attribution to a single cause is challenging, then summing two separately attributed quantities and interpreting the sum as “compound” is not automatically justified; it can embed correlated errors and conceptual overlap. This is especially problematic because the temperature component is model-attributable all-cause mortality (after exclusions), while the COVID component is certificate-coded deaths, which are not directly comparable partitions of the same outcome space. The paper does not provide sensitivity analyses showing how compound results change under alternative attribution rules (e.g., including vs excluding COVID-coded deaths in the temperature model). As a result, the compound mortality numbers may be more reflective of a chosen accounting convention than a uniquely defined epidemiologic quantity. That weakens the reliability of the main compound estimates.
```python
eng_wales_df["hw_tempcovid_death_sum_per100k"] = ((np.array(temp_hw_ds)+np.array(covid_hw_ds))/np.array(reg_pops))*100000
eng_wales_df["hw_tempcovid_low_sum_per100k"] = ((np.array(temp_hw_lw)+np.array(covid_hw_ds))/np.array(reg_pops))*100000
eng_wales_df["hw_tempcovid_hgh_sum_per100k"] = ((np.array(temp_hw_up)+np.array(covid_hw_ds))/np.array(reg_pops))*100000

eng_wales_df["cs_tempcovid_death_sum_per100k"] = ((np.array(temp_cs_ds)+np.array(covid_cs_ds))/np.array(reg_pops))*100000    
eng_wales_df["cs_tempcovid_low_sum_per100k"] = ((np.array(temp_cs_lw)+np.array(covid_cs_ds))/np.array(reg_pops))*100000
eng_wales_df["cs_tempcovid_hgh_sum_per100k"] = ((np.array(temp_cs_up)+np.array(covid_cs_ds))/np.array(reg_pops))*100000
```
The code shows the compound estimates are operationally implemented as a direct sum of the two components, with COVID deaths treated as fixed (no uncertainty) and temperature deaths contributing the only uncertainty band. This construction assumes not only independence in expectation but also ignores covariance between estimates, which is precisely what would arise if pandemic conditions modify temperature effects. It also implicitly assumes that COVID deaths are disjoint from temperature-attributable deaths, even though the manuscript acknowledges overlapping risk factors and possible amplification. Because these computed sums are the values mapped in Figure 3 and used to motivate “unprecedented compound health impact,” any conceptual mismatch directly propagates to headline results. A more defensible implementation would estimate joint models (e.g., including pandemic-period indicators and interactions) or provide decomposition bounds under alternative overlap assumptions. As-is, the computation is a major threat to internal validity.
For the specific form of Eq. (2), it should be noted that , i.e. the sum of the attributable risk measured for individual exposures is usually higher than their concurrent attributable risk. [[1]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4021419/)
This methodological point from the attributable-risk literature directly cautions against summing single-exposure attributable risks and interpreting the result as the “concurrent” burden when exposures (or risk factors) co-occur. The paper’s compound calculation does exactly this—adding separate temperature-attributable mortality and COVID-19 deaths—without establishing that the two components represent non-overlapping counterfactuals. The quote highlights that even under standard attributable-risk definitions, naive additivity tends to overstate the joint burden relative to a properly defined concurrent attributable risk. This is not a minor nuance; it can change both absolute compound numbers and the regional comparisons emphasized in the abstract. The paper would need to formalize the target estimand (e.g., joint counterfactual for temperature and COVID) to justify summation. Without that, the compound results are not clearly interpretable.
Heat waves and Covid-19 overlap, as this pandemic continues into summer 2021. [[4]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8056477/)
This sentence reflects a widely recognized premise in the public-health literature: heat events and COVID-19 can co-occur and stress the same systems and populations. Overlap implies that risks and outcomes during coincident periods are not cleanly separable into two independent components, especially when healthcare capacity and behavioral changes are part of the mechanism. The reviewed paper uses the overlap premise to motivate the study, but then treats the mortality components as independent for its primary compound calculation. This mismatch matters because the paper’s main contribution is claimed to be quantifying “compound” health consequences; overlap is precisely where interactions and shared pathways are expected. The cited review further emphasizes overlapping vulnerable groups and contradictory recommendations, reinforcing that pandemic context can change heat-health outcomes. Therefore, independence should be treated as a strong assumption requiring explicit validation or sensitivity analysis.

References:
[1] [Gasparrini A, Leone M. Attributable risk from distributed lag models. *BMC Medical Research Methodology* (2014).](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4021419/)
[4] [Bose-O’Reilly S, Daanen H, Deering K, et al. COVID-19 and heat waves: New challenges for healthcare systems. *Environmental Research* (2021).](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8056477/)

ai_review_item.review_item:
This paper draws overly strong conclusions about human safety/tolerability at 11.7T from a small, short-term, endpoint-limited protocol, which weakens the validity of the safety claims made in the Abstract and Main text.

"To assure the safety of human imaging at such high field strength, we performed physiological, vestibular, behavioral and genotoxicity measurements on the volunteers. The data shows no evidence of adverse effects." 
This pair of sentences is used to justify a broad safety conclusion, but “no evidence of adverse effects” is a non-equivalence statement that depends strongly on sample size, endpoint sensitivity, and follow-up duration. The described measures target a subset of plausible acute and subacute effects, but do not address detection of rare events or delayed outcomes, which is central when translating a first-in-human ultra-high-field exposure into a generalized “safety” assertion. The paper does not report an a priori safety endpoint hierarchy, minimally important differences, or any power/sensitivity analysis for the bioeffects outcomes, so readers cannot assess what magnitude of harm the study could have detected. In MRI safety contexts, the field’s novelty (11.7T whole-body) raises the bar for evidentiary strength, because the appropriate inference is typically “no concerning signal observed under this protocol” rather than “safe” in general. As written, the conclusion materially overreaches what the reported study design can support.
"Twenty volunteers (24.2±4.8 year old, 8 males) were scanned at 11.7T and performed the tests mentioned below." 
A cohort of 20 healthy young adults is a reasonable starting point for feasibility, but it is not sufficient to substantiate broad claims about safety and innocuousness of 11.7T scanning, particularly for outcomes that may be infrequent or heterogeneous across populations. The inclusion criteria (18–40 years) also intentionally narrow the population, which further limits external validity of the “humans can safely tolerate” implication. The manuscript does not provide a rationale that this N is adequate for the multiple physiological, vestibular, cognitive, and genotoxicity endpoints it assesses. Without a quantitative sensitivity/power discussion, the negative findings are hard to interpret because the study could simply be underpowered for clinically meaningful or rare effects. In safety-focused first-in-human work, it is standard to explicitly frame conclusions as preliminary and bounded by the sample and follow-up.
"Randomised trials offer the best approach for providing safety data as well as efficacy data, although they cannot detect rare harms." [[5]](#cite-5)
This statement highlights a core limitation of using small human studies to infer safety: even rigorous randomized designs are structurally limited in detecting rare adverse events. The present paper’s safety assessment is based on two cohorts of 20 participants and is not positioned as an adequately powered harms-detection study, making the “no adverse effects” framing particularly vulnerable to false reassurance. The issue is amplified because the paper does not quantify uncertainty around adverse-event rates (e.g., confidence bounds) or specify which harms were actively adjudicated versus passively reported. The quoted guidance is consistent with common practice that early studies can screen for frequent/large effects but cannot establish absence of uncommon harms. Therefore, the manuscript’s broad safety conclusion is not justified by its study scale.
"Authors should particularly avoid the common error of interpreting a non-significant result as indicating equivalence of interventions." [[5]](#cite-5)
The paper repeatedly interprets “no significant differences” between 11.7T and 0T as evidence for innocuousness, but the statistical non-significance of group differences does not establish equivalence without an explicit equivalence/non-inferiority design and margins. The manuscript does not report equivalence margins, confidence intervals for group differences, or an analysis plan tailored to equivalence, so its interpretation risks precisely the error described in the CONSORT elaboration. This is not a cosmetic reporting issue: it directly affects whether the central safety conclusion is logically warranted. In safety studies, equivalence-style reasoning is often crucial because the claim of “no effect” must be defined quantitatively. The absence of that framework makes the conclusion unreliable.
"Likewise, a relatively large p-value does not imply evidence in favor of the null hypothesis; many other hypotheses may be equally or more consistent with the observed data." [[2]](#cite-2)
The manuscript’s safety narrative leans heavily on finding no statistically significant differences between groups and then describing the impact of field exposure as “negligible,” but the cited principle warns against that inference. A large p-value is compatible with a wide range of effect sizes when the study is small or measurements are noisy, which is exactly the situation for many physiological and behavioral endpoints in N=20 cohorts. The paper does not present confidence intervals for key endpoints or a structured equivalence analysis, so the reader cannot tell whether clinically meaningful differences have been excluded. In MRI safety evaluation, the magnitude and uncertainty of effects matter at least as much as whether a threshold is crossed, because small effects may still accumulate or interact with other stressors. This mismatch between statistical interpretation best practices and the manuscript’s conclusions undermines the robustness of its “no adverse effects” messaging.
"A modified testing protocol informed by these results was devised with the goal of permitting increased enrollment while providing continued monitoring to evaluate field effects." [[4]](#cite-4)
This prior ultra-high-field study (10.5T) illustrates a more cautious and methodologically standard posture: initial observations are treated as preliminary signals that motivate larger enrollment and continued monitoring, rather than being used to broadly confirm safety. The contrast matters because the paper under review claims that its “safety and imaging data” confirm applicability and that “humans can safely tolerate” 11.7T, despite having a comparable or smaller cohort than other UHF safety pilots. The quoted sentence evidences a field norm that early UHF human exposure studies remain vigilant to subtle or low-frequency effects and explicitly plan follow-on monitoring to strengthen inference. That norm is especially relevant at 11.7T, where multiple physiological systems (vestibular, cognitive, cardiovascular, thermal) could be affected. Compared to this practice, the reviewed paper’s conclusion is stronger than its evidentiary base.

References:
[2] Wasserstein RL, Lazar NA. *The ASA’s statement on p-values: context, process, and purpose*. The American Statistician (2016). https://sciences.ucf.edu/biology/d4lab/wp-content/uploads/sites/23/2017/03/WassersteinLazar2016.pdf
[4] Grant A, Metzger GJ, Van de Moortele P-F, et al. *10.5 T MRI static field effects on human cognitive, vestibular, and physiological function* (Abstract). PubMed (PMID: 32822819). https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/32822819/
[5] Moher D, Hopewell S, Schulz KF, et al. *CONSORT 2010 Explanation and Elaboration: updated guidelines for reporting parallel group randomised trials* (PDF). BMJ (2010). https://www.bmj.com/content/bmj/340/bmj.c869.full.pdf

A large p-value is compatible with a wide range of effect sizes when the study is small or measurements are noisy, which is exactly the situation for many physiological and behavioral endpoints in N=20 cohorts. The paper does not present confidence intervals for key endpoints or a structured equivalence analysis, so the reader cannot tell whether clinically meaningful differences have been excluded. In MRI safety evaluation, the magnitude and uncertainty of effects matter at least as much as whether a threshold is crossed, because small effects may still accumulate or interact with other stressors. This mismatch between statistical interpretation best practices and the manuscript’s conclusions undermines the robustness of its “no adverse effects” messaging.
"A modified testing protocol informed by these results was devised with the goal of permitting increased enrollment while providing continued monitoring to evaluate field effects." [[4]](#cite-4)
This prior ultra-high-field study (10.5T) illustrates a more cautious and methodologically standard posture: initial observations are treated as preliminary signals that motivate larger enrollment and continued monitoring, rather than being used to broadly confirm safety. The contrast matters because the paper under review claims that its “safety and imaging data” confirm applicability and that “humans can safely tolerate” 11.7T, despite having a comparable or smaller cohort than other UHF safety pilots. The quoted sentence evidences a field norm that early UHF human exposure studies remain vigilant to subtle or low-frequency effects and explicitly plan follow-on monitoring to strengthen inference. That norm is especially relevant at 11.7T, where multiple physiological systems (vestibular, cognitive, cardiovascular, thermal) could be affected. Compared to this practice, the reviewed paper’s conclusion is stronger than its evidentiary base.

References:
[2] Wasserstein RL, Lazar NA. *The ASA’s statement on p-values: context, process, and purpose*. The American Statistician (2016). https://sciences.ucf.edu/biology/d4lab/wp-content/uploads/sites/23/2017/03/WassersteinLazar2016.pdf
[4] Grant A, Metzger GJ, Van de Moortele P-F, et al. *10.5 T MRI static field effects on human cognitive, vestibular, and physiological function* (Abstract). PubMed (PMID: 32822819). https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/32822819/
[5] Moher D, Hopewell S, Schulz KF, et al. *CONSORT 2010 Explanation and Elaboration: updated guidelines for reporting parallel group randomised trials* (PDF). BMJ (2010). https://www.bmj.com/content/bmj/340/bmj.c869.full.pdf

ai_review_item.review_item:
The projection of biodiversity impacts for intensification is methodologically inconsistent with the expansion scenario and contradicts the authors' claim that their models capture landscape-scale impacts. Specifically, the intensification projection weights the biodiversity change by the crop's relative area fraction, effectively treating the impact as local to the crop's footprint, whereas the models (based on generic "Cropland" data and landscape-scale yields) and the expansion scenario imply landscape-wide effects. This likely underestimates the biodiversity cost of intensification.

"Therefore, our models capture landscape-scale but not local-scale impacts of crop-specific management."
The authors explicitly state that their models capture landscape-scale impacts, implying that the yield variable (measured at ~10km resolution) influences biodiversity across the landscape (or at least across all cropland in the landscape), not just within the specific crop's fields.
`MaizeSR$SR_1perIntensification_maize <- MaizeSR$PV_1perIntensification_RelFractions_maize * ((MaizeSR$PV_1perIntensification_BiodivPred_maize * MaizeSR$PV_1perIntensification_primVegPer_maize) + (MaizeSR$CL_1perIntensification_BiodivPred_maize * MaizeSR$CL_1perIntensification_CroplandPer_maize)) + (1-MaizeSR$PV_1perIntensification_RelFractions_maize) * MaizeSR$SR_noChange_maize`
In the code for intensification, the new biodiversity state (calculated using the increased yield) is weighted by `RelFractions` (the crop's share of agricultural area), while the remaining portion of the pixel is assumed to stay at the "No Change" state. This weighting treats the effect of increased yield as being confined to the specific crop's area, which contradicts the "landscape-scale" interpretation of the model. If the model predicts landscape-scale biodiversity based on landscape-scale yield, increasing the yield should affect the entire relevant land use (e.g., all cropland), not just a fraction.
`MaizeSR$SR_1perExpansion_maize <- (MaizeSR$PV_1perExpansion_BiodivPred_maize * MaizeSR$PV_1perExpansion_primVegPer_maize) + (MaizeSR$CL_1perExpansion_BiodivPred_maize * MaizeSR$CL_1perExpansion_CroplandPer_maize)`
In contrast, the expansion scenario calculates the new biodiversity state for the entire pixel (based on new land use proportions) without any such weighting by the crop's relative fraction. This inconsistency biases the comparison in favor of intensification by dampening its estimated impact.

ai_review_item.review_item:
The paper claims that the neural network results for the homogeneous electron gas (HEG) "outperform many traditional ab initio methods" and achieve "error of less than 1%" using BF-DMC as reference. However, subsequent independent analysis in the literature demonstrates that the DeepSolid method (referred to as "LiNet") does not actually improve upon backflow DMC (BF-DMC) results across the full density regime for N=54 electrons, undermining the claimed superiority of the method for this benchmark system.

"Overall, our neural network performs very well, with an error of less than 1% in a wide range of density (see Supplementary Table 11)."
This claim presents the correlation error relative to BF-DMC, but the framing suggests the neural network method is achieving higher accuracy than traditional methods. A subsequent study by Pescia et al. (2024) in Physical Review B directly compared DeepSolid (labeled "LiNet") against BF-DMC for N=54 electrons and found that "This is in stark contrast to the (FermiNet-based) architecture of [27] (dubbed LiNet in the following), which does not improve upon BF-DMC energies over the whole density regime" [[1]](#citation-1). This directly contradicts the paper's implication of superior performance.
"the obtained results, e.g. total energies, dissociation curves, and cohesive energies, outperform many traditional ab initio methods and reach the level of the most accurate approaches"
The abstract makes a broad claim of outperforming "many traditional ab initio methods." However, for HEG specifically, the neural network VMC results remain at or slightly above BF-DMC energies due to the inherent variational bias. The paper's HEG Table 11 in the supplementary material shows energies that are systematically higher (less accurate) than BF-DMC at most densities, which is expected for a variational method but contradicts claims of "outperforming" state-of-the-art approaches.
"The state-of-the-art results, namely VMC with backflow correlation (BF) [22] and distinguishable cluster with double excitations (DCD) [23] are also plotted for comparison, and the most accurate BF-DMC result is used as the reference energy of correlation error."
By using BF-DMC as the reference and only comparing against BF-VMC and DCD, the paper obscures the fact that the neural network VMC cannot provide energies lower than BF-DMC due to the variational principle. The message-passing NQS study [[1]](#citation-1) shows that more recent neural network approaches can achieve lower energies than DeepSolid while using fewer parameters, suggesting the DeepSolid architecture is not optimal for this system.

References:
[1] Pescia, G., Nys, J., Kim, J., Lovato, A. & Carleo, G. Message-Passing Neural Quantum States for the Homogeneous Electron Gas. [Phys. Rev. B 110, 035108 (2024)](https://journals.aps.org/prb/abstract/10.1103/PhysRevB.110.035108); arXiv:2305.07240.

ai_review_item.review_item:
The paper uses frozen polyBERT fingerprints (without fine-tuning the transformer weights) for property prediction, which deviates from established practices in chemical language modeling where fine-tuning typically improves downstream task performance.

"polyBERT fingerprints (dashed arrow) are the averages over the token dimension (sentence average) of the last Transformer encoder."
The polyBERT fingerprints are extracted as fixed representations from the pre-trained model, with no indication that the transformer weights are updated during property prediction. This means the 600-dimensional fingerprint vectors are computed once and remain static, while only the downstream multitask neural network is trained. This approach may not fully leverage the power of transfer learning, as the pre-trained representations cannot adapt to capture property-specific patterns that may be critical for accurate prediction.
"Built upon the RoBERTa architecture and pre-trained on 77 million SMILES strings from PubChem, ChemBERTa learns rich chemical representations without requiring labelled data... These achievements have inspired researchers to explore the application of transformer architectures to chemical domains." [[4]](#citation-list)
The ChemBERTa paper and subsequent work in chemical language modeling typically employ fine-tuning for downstream property prediction tasks, allowing the model to adapt its learned representations to the specific prediction target. The polyBERT paper's exclusive use of frozen fingerprints represents a methodological choice that may underestimate the full potential of the transformer-based approach, and the comparison with Polymer Genome fingerprints may not reflect the performance achievable through end-to-end fine-tuning.
"MI-MoE-SchNet achieves the best overall performance, substantially reducing RMSE on all four properties and outperforming both traditional baselines and strong geometry-aware models... In contrast, sequence-based and graph-based baselines such as polyBERT, TransPolymer, and polyGNN exhibit comparatively higher errors, especially on structure-sensitive properties." [[1]](#citation-list)
A recent benchmark study comparing multiple polymer property prediction methods found that polyBERT exhibited comparatively higher errors than more recent methods that incorporate fine-tuning or geometric information. For example, on crystallization tendency (Xc), polyBERT achieved RMSE of 17.646 compared to 8.945 for the best-performing method. This suggests that the frozen fingerprint approach may not capture all the information relevant for accurate property prediction, particularly for structure-sensitive properties.

References:
[1] [Topology-Aware Multiscale Mixture of Experts for Efficient Molecular Property Prediction](https://arxiv.org/html/2601.12637v1) - arXiv (2025)
[4] [Transformers in drug discovery: fine-tuning ChemBERTa for molecular property prediction](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S1359644626000073) - Drug Discovery Today (2026)

ai_review_item.review_item: This paper’s reported SHS27K protein–protein interaction (PPI) gains are not a valid estimate of generalization because the PPI evaluation lacks a validation split and the released code uses the test split for model selection/early stopping and for pseudo-edge augmentation, directly undermining the paper’s state-of-the-art performance claims. "Specifically, on the PPI dataset, MUSE outperformed all existing models including single-scale (DrugVQA [8] and TAG-PPI [25]), and multi-view methods (GNN-PPI [22] and HIGH-PPI [15]). TAG-PPI and DrugVQA, which solely focus on the atomic structure scale, achieved the poorest performance as they neglected the molecular network scale information. Our model showed substantial improvements over the strongest baseline HIGH-PPI, which also incorporates multi-scale information for enhanced predictions, with an increase of 8.41% in the BFS split, 13.04% in the DFS split, 7.69% in the Random split, (Fig. 2c and Fig. S1)." This passage is the paper’s central empirical claim for multi-scale interaction prediction on SHS27K, asserting large improvements over the strongest baseline across three splits. Such claims require a strictly held-out test set so that the reported numbers reflect generalization rather than adaptation to the evaluation labels. Because the magnitude of the claimed gains (e.g., 13.04% on DFS) is used to justify the overall superiority of the EM framework, any test-set contamination directly affects the paper’s conclusions. In common ML practice, test labels must not influence checkpoint selection, early stopping, threshold tuning, or graph augmentation decisions. Therefore, if the experimental protocol does not isolate the test set, this specific performance claim becomes unreliable as evidence of a better method. "Random\n 5328\n -\n 1332" This row from Table S1 shows that, for SHS27K under the Random split, the Validation column is explicitly marked as "-", i.e., no validation set is used. Without a validation split, the usual mechanisms for selecting hyperparameters, choosing the best epoch, and early stopping cannot be performed without touching the test set. In graph learning papers, it is standard to reserve validation data for model selection precisely to avoid optimistic bias in the final reported test numbers. The absence of validation is especially problematic here because the method is iterative (multi-iteration EM), which increases the number of opportunities to inadvertently tune toward the evaluation set. Thus, the table itself signals a high risk that the test set may be used as a surrogate validation set. ```python class PPILinkTrainer: def create_dataloaders(self): split_edges = self.dataset.get_edge_split() return split_edges['train'], split_edges['test'] def train(self, gnn_model=None, protein_dataset=None, protein_embeddings=None, em_iter=0): ... for self.cur_epoch in range(self.run_config.link_epochs): ... valid_loss, test_metrics = self.evaluate(self.graph, self.test_edges, self.test_label) ... if best_valid_f1 < test_metrics.F1: best_valid_f1 = test_metrics.F1 ... torch.save({...}, os.path.join(self.args.res_dir, f'link_{em_iter}_best_model.ckpt')) ... if stopper > self.patience: write_log(self.logging, "Early Stopping.") break ``` This code makes the test split ("split_edges['test']") the only non-training split used inside training, and then evaluates on it every epoch to choose the best checkpoint (via best_valid_f1). That means test labels directly control early stopping and model selection, which invalidates the test set as an unbiased estimator of generalization. The problem is not merely a naming issue: the model is explicitly saved based on the best score computed on the held-out split it later reports as "Validation_avg" in logs. This creates systematic optimistic bias because the training loop can select epochs that happen to perform well on that same evaluation set. In standard benchmarking, the test set should be evaluated once after all training decisions are fixed using only training/validation data. Consequently, the reported SHS27K performance can be inflated by construction. ```python if gnn_model is not None: pseudo_edges = self.gnn_inference(gnn_model, protein_dataset) self.graph.edge_index = torch.cat([self.graph.edge_index, pseudo_edges.to(self.device)], dim=1) ... def gnn_inference(self, gnn_model, protein_dataset): ... num_batches = int(len(self.test_edges) // batch_size) for batch_index in range(num_batches): edges = self.test_edges[batch_index*batch_size : (batch_index+1)*batch_size] ... ... pseudo_edges = self.test_edges[pseudo_edges_idx].squeeze(1).t() ... return pseudo_edges ``` Here the model explicitly constructs pseudo edges from self.test_edges and injects them into the training graph via concatenation to self.graph.edge_index. Even if the pseudo labels are predicted (rather than ground-truth), this is still a form of transductive adaptation to the evaluation set because the held-out edges are being added into the graph used for message passing and subsequent optimization. Since the same test_edges are later evaluated for performance, the procedure risks a circular evaluation in which the model is trained on (or structurally conditioned on) the very edges it is asked to predict. This is materially different from using only the training graph plus unlabeled nodes, as it directly exposes the model to the test edge list during training. Such leakage is especially consequential in link prediction, where the graph structure is itself the primary signal. As a result, the evaluation no longer cleanly measures predictive performance on unseen interactions. "Such experimental setup favors the model that overﬁts the most and defeats the main purpose of using a train/validation/test split — ﬁnding the model with the best generalization properties [Friedman et al., 2001]." [[2]](#ref2) This statement from the graph ML evaluation literature explains why the train/validation/test separation exists: to select models based on validation performance while preserving a clean estimate of generalization on the test set. The paper under review effectively collapses validation into test for SHS27K (as evidenced by Table S1 and the training code), which is exactly the kind of setup criticized here. The issue is not cosmetic; it changes which model is selected and can change the apparent ranking of methods. This matters particularly for iterative training paradigms (like EM-style alternation) where repeated evaluation increases the chance of selecting a checkpoint that performs well on the test split by chance. The quoted guidance reflects a widely accepted standard for fair comparisons across architectures and training procedures. Therefore, by violating this principle, the paper’s SHS27K PPI claims do not meet common validity expectations. "Failure to identify the sources of empirical gains, e.g. emphasizing unnecessary modiﬁcations to neural architectures when gains actually stem from hyper-parameter tuning." [[5]](#ref5) This observation is relevant because the paper attributes improvements to its EM-based multi-scale training paradigm and mutual supervision, but the code reveals additional evaluation-coupled choices (e.g., selecting checkpoints by test performance and augmenting with test edges). When evaluation leakage exists, it becomes impossible to disentangle whether reported gains come from the proposed methodological contribution or from training/evaluation artifacts. In other words, the experimental setup itself can become a confounder that produces performance gains unrelated to the core algorithmic idea. In fields like graph link prediction, subtle changes to data splits and selection criteria are known to materially change results, so rigor in isolating sources of gains is essential. The quoted critique highlights why ablation and proper selection protocols are required to support causal claims about what made performance improve. Thus, the paper’s attribution of gains to the EM framework is not well-supported under the observed evaluation procedure. References: [2] [Oleksandr Shchur et al., "Pitfalls of Graph Neural Network Evaluation" (NeurIPS 2018 Workshop).](https://arxiv.org/abs/1811.05868) [5] [Zachary C. Lipton and Jacob Steinhardt, "Troubling Trends in Machine Learning Scholarship" (2018).](https://arxiv.org/abs/1807.03341)

Consequently, the reported SHS27K performance can be inflated by construction. ```python if gnn_model is not None: pseudo_edges = self.gnn_inference(gnn_model, protein_dataset) self.graph.edge_index = torch.cat([self.graph.edge_index, pseudo_edges.to(self.device)], dim=1) ... def gnn_inference(self, gnn_model, protein_dataset): ... num_batches = int(len(self.test_edges) // batch_size) for batch_index in range(num_batches): edges = self.test_edges[batch_index*batch_size : (batch_index+1)*batch_size] ... ... pseudo_edges = self.test_edges[pseudo_edges_idx].squeeze(1).t() ... return pseudo_edges ``` Here the model explicitly constructs pseudo edges from self.test_edges and injects them into the training graph via concatenation to self.graph.edge_index. Even if the pseudo labels are predicted (rather than ground-truth), this is still a form of transductive adaptation to the evaluation set because the held-out edges are being added into the graph used for message passing and subsequent optimization. Since the same test_edges are later evaluated for performance, the procedure risks a circular evaluation in which the model is trained on (or structurally conditioned on) the very edges it is asked to predict. This is materially different from using only the training graph plus unlabeled nodes, as it directly exposes the model to the test edge list during training. Such leakage is especially consequential in link prediction, where the graph structure is itself the primary signal. As a result, the evaluation no longer cleanly measures predictive performance on unseen interactions. "Such experimental setup favors the model that overﬁts the most and defeats the main purpose of using a train/validation/test split — ﬁnding the model with the best generalization properties [Friedman et al., 2001]." [[2]](#ref2) This statement from the graph ML evaluation literature explains why the train/validation/test separation exists: to select models based on validation performance while preserving a clean estimate of generalization on the test set. The paper under review effectively collapses validation into test for SHS27K (as evidenced by Table S1 and the training code), which is exactly the kind of setup criticized here. The issue is not cosmetic; it changes which model is selected and can change the apparent ranking of methods. This matters particularly for iterative training paradigms (like EM-style alternation) where repeated evaluation increases the chance of selecting a checkpoint that performs well on the test split by chance. The quoted guidance reflects a widely accepted standard for fair comparisons across architectures and training procedures. Therefore, by violating this principle, the paper’s SHS27K PPI claims do not meet common validity expectations. "Failure to identify the sources of empirical gains, e.g. emphasizing unnecessary modiﬁcations to neural architectures when gains actually stem from hyper-parameter tuning." [[5]](#ref5) This observation is relevant because the paper attributes improvements to its EM-based multi-scale training paradigm and mutual supervision, but the code reveals additional evaluation-coupled choices (e.g., selecting checkpoints by test performance and augmenting with test edges). When evaluation leakage exists, it becomes impossible to disentangle whether reported gains come from the proposed methodological contribution or from training/evaluation artifacts. In other words, the experimental setup itself can become a confounder that produces performance gains unrelated to the core algorithmic idea. In fields like graph link prediction, subtle changes to data splits and selection criteria are known to materially change results, so rigor in isolating sources of gains is essential. The quoted critique highlights why ablation and proper selection protocols are required to support causal claims about what made performance improve. Thus, the paper’s attribution of gains to the EM framework is not well-supported under the observed evaluation procedure. References: [2] [Oleksandr Shchur et al., "Pitfalls of Graph Neural Network Evaluation" (NeurIPS 2018 Workshop).](https://arxiv.org/abs/1811.05868) [5] [Zachary C. Lipton and Jacob Steinhardt, "Troubling Trends in Machine Learning Scholarship" (2018).](https://arxiv.org/abs/1807.03341)
